mnemonics.title: HEART Framework
mnemonics.content: Remember HEART: Happiness - how happy are users? Engagement - how much do they use it? Adoption - new users coming? Retention - do they stay? Task success - can they do what they want?

mnemonics.title: SMART Goals
mnemonics.content: SMART = Specific (clear goal), Measurable (can track), Achievable (realistic), Relevant (matters), Time-bound (deadline). Like a GPS for design projects!

mnemonics.title: UX Metrics Categories
mnemonics.content: Remember 3Es of UX Metrics: Effectiveness (can they finish task?), Efficiency (how fast?), Emotional response (do they like it?)

acronyms.expansion: Happiness, Engagement, Adoption, Retention, Task success
acronyms.usage: Framework for measuring user experience quality
Measures user loyalty by likelihood to recommend (range -100 to +100)

acronyms.expansion: Daily Active Users / Monthly Active Users
acronyms.usage: Measures user engagement and retention for apps
Critical metrics tracking success of design objectives
Pirate metrics funnel for product growth

visualTips.visualization: Picture a heart shape with 5 chambers, each labeled H-E-A-R-T. Each chamber glows based on metric performance - bright means good, dim means needs work.
Imagine a shopping funnel - wide at top (all visitors), narrowing at each step (cart, checkout, purchase). Mark where most users drop off.
Think of a SMART goal as a smart robot with specific eyes (Specific), measuring tape (Measurable), rocket (Achievable), target board (Relevant), and clock (Time-bound).

analogies.analogy: Metrics are like speedometers in a car (showing current speed), while targets are the speed limit signs (showing where you should be). Both are needed for safe driving!
Bounce rate is like customers walking into a store and immediately walking out without looking at anything - they didn't find what they needed.
Like a GPS success rate - if 9 out of 10 drivers reach their destination using your GPS, that's 90% task completion.
Churn rate is like a subscription magazine - if 20% of subscribers cancel each month, that's your monthly churn rate.
A/B testing is like a science experiment - showing two different book covers to different groups to see which gets more readers.
